# Deployment

## Pipeline overview

```
git push → Cloud Build trigger
  → Step 1: docker build (node:lts-alpine → npm install → CMD yarn start:${NODE_ENV})
  → Step 2: docker push → Artifact Registry
  → Step 3: gcloud run deploy → Cloud Run
  → container start: models/db.js runs inline DB migrations
```

No GitHub Actions. CI/CD is handled entirely by Google Cloud Build (`cloudbuild.yaml`).

## Dockerfile

- Base: `node:lts-alpine`
- Installs ImageMagick (for image processing endpoints)
- Copies source, runs `yarn install` (includes devDependencies for `cross-env` and `nodemon`)
- Exposes port 5000
- CMD: `yarn start:$NODE_ENV` — uses the start script matching the injected `NODE_ENV`

The backend Docker image does **not** run tests at build time (unlike the frontend), and there is no separate GitHub Actions CI. Tests gate startup only for the `start:sandbox` and `start:production` scripts (which prefix `npm test &&`); `start:sandbox-albert` runs `nodemon` with no test gate. Run `npm test` locally before pushing. See [`development.md`](development.md).

## Startup migrations

There is no separate migration job. On **every container start**, `models/db.js` (loaded via `index.js`) runs a sequence of idempotent inline migrations against the database — `ALTER TABLE "RFQItem"/"Factory"/"users"/"customers" ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS ...`, `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS app_settings`, and a `Factory.factory_country` backfill `UPDATE`. A schema change ships by adding an `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` statement to `models/db.js`; the new column is only realized in an environment once the new revision boots.

**Mixed-case column identifiers MUST be double-quoted** in these statements. Sequelize quotes all identifiers; Postgres folds unquoted ones to lowercase. An unquoted `ADD COLUMN` created an orphan `rfqitem_price_sales_snapshots` that broke the RFQ grid (SYS-002, fixed in `a556d3f`). See AGENTS.md §14.

## Image tag format

```
{REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/{PROJECT_ID}/{ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME}/{APP_ENV}/{APP_NAME}:{BUILD_ID}-{COMMIT_SHA}
```

## Cloud Run service

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Service name | `popcre-albert-backend-sandbox` (sandbox-albert env) |
| GCP project | `lithe-breaker-323913` |
| Region | `us-central1` |
| Auth | `--no-allow-unauthenticated` (requests authenticated by BFF with OIDC token) |
| VPC connector | configured via `_VPC_CONNECTOR` trigger variable |

Secrets are injected at deploy time via `--set-secrets`; non-secret config via `--set-env-vars`. See [`configuration.md`](configuration.md) for the full variable list.

## Triggering a build

The Cloud Build trigger `popcre-albert-backend-sandbox` is configured (in the Google Cloud Console) to fire on push to the `sandbox-albert` branch and auto-deploys the new revision. (The Cloud Run service for this env happens to share the same name.) Each environment has its own trigger with its own substitution variables.

## Environments

| Environment | Branch | Cloud Run service | Domain | Purpose |
|-------------|--------|-------------------|--------|---------|
| `sandbox-albert` | `sandbox-albert` | `popcre-albert-backend-sandbox` | `api.core.alsand.designflow.app` | Albert's personal dev/test |
| `sandbox` | `develop` | `popcre-core-sandbox` | `api.core.sandbox.designflow.app` | Shared staging |
| `production` | `main` | `popcre-core-prod` | `api.core.designflow.app` | Live production |

**Note on domain names vs OIDC:** The domain column is for display/routing only. BFF services always call backends using the `.run.app` service URL as both the proxy target and OIDC token audience — not the custom domain. See the BFF repo `AGENTS.md` for the full explanation.

## Rollback

```bash
# List revisions
gcloud run revisions list \
  --service=popcre-albert-backend-sandbox \
  --region=us-central1 \
  --project=lithe-breaker-323913

# Shift 100% traffic to a prior revision
gcloud run services update-traffic popcre-albert-backend-sandbox \
  --region=us-central1 \
  --project=lithe-breaker-323913 \
  --to-revisions=<REVISION_NAME>=100
```

## Viewing build logs

Cloud Build uses `logging: CLOUD_LOGGING_ONLY`. Retrieve logs via Cloud Logging:

```bash
# Find recent build IDs
gcloud logging read "resource.type=build" \
  --project=lithe-breaker-323913 --limit=5 --format=json

# Stream full log for a specific build
gcloud logging read "resource.labels.build_id=<BUILD_ID>" \
  --project=lithe-breaker-323913 --limit=200 \
  --format="value(textPayload)"
```

## Service account requirements

The Cloud Run service account `deployer@lithe-breaker-323913.iam.gserviceaccount.com` must have `roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor` on every secret listed in `--set-secrets`. Granting access to a new secret is part of the [secret creation procedure](configuration.md#adding-a-new-secret).

---
name: "yeet"
description: "Publish local changes to GitHub by confirming scope, committing intentionally, pushing the branch, and opening a draft PR through the GitHub app from this plugin, with `gh` used only as a fallback where connector coverage is insufficient."
---

# GitHub Publish Changes

## Overview

Use this skill only when the user explicitly wants the full publish flow from the local checkout: branch setup if needed, staging, commit, push, and opening a pull request.

This workflow is hybrid:

- Use local `git` for branch creation, staging, commit, and push.
- Prefer the GitHub app from this plugin for pull request creation after the branch is on the remote.
- Use `gh` as a fallback for current-branch PR discovery, auth checks, or PR creation when the connector path cannot infer the repository or head branch cleanly.

## Prerequisites

- Require GitHub CLI `gh`. Check `gh --version`. If missing, ask the user to install `gh` and stop.
- Require authenticated `gh` session. Run `gh auth status`. If not authenticated, ask the user to run `gh auth login` (and re-run `gh auth status`) before continuing.
- Require a local git repository with a clean understanding of which changes belong in the PR.

## Naming conventions

- Branch: `codex/{description}` when starting from main/master/default.
- Commit: `{description}` (terse).
- PR title: `[codex] {description}` summarizing the full diff.

## Workflow

1. Confirm intended scope.
   - Run `git status -sb` and inspect the diff before staging.
   - If the working tree contains unrelated changes, do not default to `git add -A`. Ask the user which files belong in the PR.
2. Determine the branch strategy.
   - If on `main`, `master`, or another default branch, create `codex/{description}`.
   - Otherwise stay on the current branch.
3. Stage only the intended changes.
   - Prefer explicit file paths when the worktree is mixed.
   - Use `git add -A` only when the user has confirmed the whole worktree belongs in scope.
4. Commit tersely with the confirmed description.
5. Run the most relevant checks available if they have not already been run.
   - If checks fail due to missing dependencies or tools, install what is needed and rerun once.
6. Push with tracking: `git push -u origin $(git branch --show-current)`.
7. Open a draft PR.
   - Prefer the GitHub app from this plugin for PR creation after the push succeeds.
   - Derive `repository_full_name` from the remote, for example by normalizing `git remote get-url origin` or by using `gh repo view --json nameWithOwner`.
   - Derive `head_branch` from `git branch --show-current`.
   - Derive `base_branch` from the user request when specified; otherwise use the remote default branch, for example via `gh repo view --json defaultBranchRef`.
   - If the branch is being pushed from a fork or the PR target differs from the remote that was just pushed, prefer `gh pr create` fallback because the connector PR creation flow expects one repository target and may not encode cross-repo head semantics cleanly.
   - If connector-based PR creation cannot infer the repository or branch cleanly, fall back to `gh pr create --draft --fill --head $(git branch --show-current)`.
   - Write the PR body to a temp file with real newlines when using CLI fallback so the markdown renders cleanly.
8. Summarize the result with branch name, commit, PR target, validation, and anything the user still needs to confirm.

## Write Safety

- Never stage unrelated user changes silently.
- Never push without confirming scope when the worktree is mixed.
- Default to a draft PR unless the user explicitly asks for a ready-for-review PR.
- If the repository does not appear to be connected to an accessible GitHub remote, stop and explain the blocker before making assumptions.

## PR Body Expectations

The PR description should use real Markdown prose and cover:

- what changed
- why it changed
- the user or developer impact
- the root cause when the PR is a fix
- the checks used to validate it

{"command":"$body = @"
## Summary
- restore RFQ detail-grid row selection setup before grid initialization
- remove stale backend-loaded colDef checkbox flags from the RFQ factory sub-grid so AG Grid v35 renders only one selection checkbox column
- add regression coverage for legacy detail layout checkbox flags

## Root Cause
The RFQ detail grid is backend-layout driven. After moving row selection to the AG Grid v35 `rowSelection` API, stale legacy `checkboxSelection` / `headerCheckboxSelection` flags could still arrive on detail column definitions, causing a second checkbox to render for factory rows.

## Validation
- `node node_modules\\jest\\bin\\jest.js --testPathPatterns="detail-cell-renderer.component.spec"`
- `node node_modules\\jest\\bin\\jest.js`
"@
gh pr create --draft --base develop --head sandbox-albert --title "fix(rfq): remove duplicate detail grid checkboxes" --body $body","workdir":"C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-frontend","timeout_ms":120000}

## Summary
- restore RFQ detail-grid row selection setup before grid initialization
- remove stale backend-loaded colDef checkbox flags from the RFQ factory sub-grid so AG Grid v35 renders only one selection checkbox column
- add regression coverage for legacy detail layout checkbox flags

## Root Cause
The RFQ detail grid is backend-layout driven. After moving row selection to the AG Grid v35 `rowSelection` API, stale legacy `checkboxSelection` / `headerCheckboxSelection` flags could still arrive on detail column definitions, causing a second checkbox to render for factory rows.
